fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a small utility layer for our PHP libraries so they can announce deprecated code paths without stepping on the host application's toes. The core idea is that a library just declares 'this thing is deprecated' with a stable URL and a message, and the host app decides what to do with it — or nothing at all if it hasn't opted in. Right now our libraries are either throwing errors directly or spamming logs in ways consumers can't control, and we keep getting complaints about noise in test suites and production logs.

The layer should support a few different modes of operation — counting only, routing to the runtime error channel, or sending to a structured logger — and the host should be able to flip between these either in code or via some kind of environment config (think the same pattern we used in that deployment-selector mechanism from the auth service refactor).

There should also be a way to silence specific deprecations or whole vendor packages without affecting whether they get counted. And we need the repeated-trigger noise problem handled by default, with an escape hatch to turn that off.

One tricky bit: some deprecations should only fire when a consumer outside the owning package calls into it — internal calls within the same package should stay quiet. Also need a reset/disable capability so test suites can clean up state between runs. Please keep the internals cleanly separated — routing, counting, and suppression logic should not be tangled together.

One extra detail that came up: if nobody has selected an activation mode yet, either in code or through the environment selector, the manager should be a full no-op. In that state, triggering a deprecation should neither record a counter nor notify any backend, and the summary should come out as unique_count=0 followed by tracked=(none). Related to activation, the enable field should be able to take a list of modes so we can turn on more than one at the same time, like 'track' plus 'trigger' or 'track' plus 'psr'. Also, the 'env' field in the JSON scenario input accepts a string value ('track' or 'trigger') to activate a mode without programmatic calls — see feature6_env_activation.json and the env activation logic in the execution adapter. One important behavioral point here: once any mode is enabled, counting is on as well.

On the tracking side, when 'track' mode is enabled, every trigger should increment a per-identifier counter keyed by the deprecation's link (URL identifier). The unique_count in the summary is the sum of ALL per-identifier counters, not the number of distinct identifiers, so repeated hits to the same identifier keep growing that identifier's count independently. The identifiers themselves should still be listed once each, in first-seen order. Deduplication should stay ON by default, which means the backend only gets notified on the FIRST occurrence of each identifier within a run. After that, later occurrences still increment the counter but do not produce another notification block. If we want a notification every single time, deduplication has to be explicitly set to false.

Also worth spelling out the message behavior a little more clearly: the message field is a printf-style template, and any trailing args values are substituted before the message reaches a backend. %s consumes a string argument, %d consumes an integer argument, and multiple placeholders consume multiple arguments in order. If there are no placeholders, the template is emitted verbatim. Whatever that formatted result is, that's what should show up in the notice block's message field.

For reset behavior, disabling the manager should both turn tracking off and reset all per-identifier counters to zero. The key nuance is that identifiers already seen during the run should still remain listed in the summary, still in first-seen order, just with count=0 each, and unique_count should drop back to zero. That's the cleanup behavior tests were asking for. The 'disable_after' boolean field in the scenario input should trigger that disable+reset behavior described in feature8_disable_reset.json: counters are zeroed but tracked identifiers remain listed with count=0.

Last formatting note on the output: the summary always comes after all notice blocks. It starts with a line exactly like 'unique_count=<N>'. If any identifiers were tracked, then we print one 'count[<identifier>]=<n>' line per tracked identifier in first-seen order. If nothing was tracked, or everything got suppressed via ignore_links, then instead of count lines we print a single line 'tracked=(none)'. The one exception is after a disable/reset: in that case, previously tracked identifiers should still be shown with count=0, so tracked=(none) is NOT used there.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The trigger_error backend emits a notice block with exactly these lines in order: event=deprecation_notice, backend=trigger_error, message=<formatted message>, package=<package>, link=<identifier>. No call-site file, line number, or other host-runtime detail appears in the observable output.

The structured-logger (psr) backend emits a notice block with exactly these lines in order: event=deprecation_notice, backend=psr_logger, message=<formatted message>, package=<package>, link=<identifier>. The package and identifier are carried as structured metadata, not appended to the message text. The observable fields are identical to the trigger_error backend except the backend label.

When a link (identifier URL) is listed in ignore_links, that deprecation is skipped entirely BEFORE any counting takes place. It is neither counted nor sent to any backend — as if it had never been triggered. The summary will show unique_count=0 and tracked=(none) if all triggers were suppressed this way.

Silencing a package via ignore_packages suppresses ONLY backend notifications for deprecations from that package; counting is unaffected. The deprecation is still counted (its counter increments and it appears in the summary), but no notice block is emitted for it.

The environment selector supports exactly two meaningful values: 'track' (enables counting only, no backend notification) and 'trigger' (enables the error-trigger backend and counting). Any other value or an absent selector leaves the manager a no-op. The observable result is identical to activating the corresponding mode programmatically. The 'psr' mode is not available via the environment selector.

A deprecation can be declared to fire only when invoked from OUTSIDE the package that owns it. The decision is based on the immediate caller's location relative to the owning package boundary. When the caller is outside (consumer code), the deprecation fires normally. When the caller is inside the same package (the package calling its own deprecated path), the trigger is silent — not counted, not notified. The fixed scenario uses package='acme/widgets', link='https://deprecations.example.com/widgets/42', message='oldWidget() is deprecated, use newWidget() instead.'.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: some deprecations should only fire when a consumer outside the owning package calls into it
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The 'from_outside' scenario selector field (values: 'outside' or 'inside') in feature7_called_from_outside.json, which simulates the triggerIfCalledFromOutside() mechanism using a fixed package/link/message combination

context_pointers.vague_reference: routing, counting, and suppression logic should not be tangled together
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The architectural constraint requiring separate modules/classes for: (1) activation/configuration, (2) counting/state management, (3) suppression rules (ignore_links, ignore_packages), and (4) backend notification (trigger_error, psr_logger) — with backends behind a common abstraction interface

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can clarify directly — you'll need to explore the existing codebase structure and test cases to infer the expected behavior. Check the test JSON files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the observable contract, and the start.md PRD for architectural guidance.

feature1_1_default_noop.json.description: When no activation mode has been selected, the manager is a complete no-op: triggering a deprecation neither records a counter nor notifies any backend. The unique total stays zero and no identifiers are tracked.

feature1_2_tracking_count.json.description: With tracking activated, every trigger increments a per-identifier counter. The unique total equals the sum of all counters. Repeated triggers of the same identifier grow its counter; distinct identifiers are counted independently in first-seen order.

feature2_1_deduplication.json.description: By default deduplication is on: the backend (here the error-trigger notifier) is invoked only on the first occurrence of each identifier within a run, even though the per-identifier counter keeps incrementing on every occurrence.

cases.expected_output:
event=deprecation_notice
backend=trigger_error
message=this is deprecated foo 1234
package=acme/orm
link=https://dep.example.com/orm/1234
unique_count=2
count[https://dep.example.com/orm/1234]=2

feature2_2_without_deduplication.json.description: When deduplication is turned off, the backend is notified on every occurrence of an identifier, not just the first. Two triggers of the same identifier produce two notifications and a counter of two.

cases.expected_output:
event=deprecation_notice
backend=trigger_error
message=this is deprecated foo 2222
package=acme/orm
link=https://dep.example.com/orm/2222
event=deprecation_notice
backend=trigger_error
message=this is deprecated foo 2222
package=acme/orm
link=https://dep.example.com/orm/2222
unique_count=2
count[https://dep.example.com/orm/2222]=2

feature3_message_formatting.json.description: The message is a printf-style template; any trailing arguments are substituted into it before it reaches a backend. Supports string and integer placeholders, multiple placeholders, and templates with no placeholders (emitted verbatim).

cases.expected_output:
event=deprecation_notice
backend=trigger_error
message=this is deprecated foo 1234
package=acme/orm
link=https://dep.example.com/orm/1234
unique_count=1
count[https://dep.example.com/orm/1234]=1

event=deprecation_notice
backend=trigger_error
message=use newApi instead
package=acme/orm
link=https://dep.example.com/orm/1234
unique_count=1
count[https://dep.example.com/orm/1234]=1

feature4_1_trigger_error_backend.json.description: The error-trigger backend emits a deprecation notice carrying the formatted message together with the originating package and the deprecation identifier. Host-specific call-site location details are not part of the observable contract.

cases.expected_output:
event=deprecation_notice
backend=trigger_error
message=this is deprecated foo 1234
package=acme/orm
link=https://dep.example.com/orm/1234
unique_count=1
count[https://dep.example.com/orm/1234]=1

feature4_2_psr_logger_backend.json.description: The structured-logger backend emits the formatted message as a clean log entry, carrying the originating package and deprecation identifier as structured metadata rather than appending them to the message text.

cases.expected_output:
event=deprecation_notice
backend=psr_logger
message=this is deprecated foo 1234
package=acme/orm
link=https://dep.example.com/orm/2222
unique_count=1
count[https://dep.example.com/orm/2222]=1

feature5_1_ignore_identifier.json.description: An identifier can be suppressed entirely. A suppressed identifier is skipped before any counting takes place: it is neither counted nor sent to any backend, as if it had never been triggered.

feature5_2_ignore_package.json.description: A whole package can be silenced from backend notifications. Deprecations from a silenced package are still counted (tracking is unaffected), but no backend notification is emitted for them.

feature6_env_activation.json.description: Activation can be driven by an environment selector instead of explicit calls. The selector value 'track' enables counting only; 'trigger' enables the error-trigger backend; any other/absent value leaves the manager a no-op.

cases.expected_output:
event=deprecation_notice
backend=trigger_error
message=message
package=acme/foo
link=https://dep.example.com/foo/1
unique_count=1
count[https://dep.example.com/foo/1]=1

feature7_called_from_outside.json.description: A deprecation can be declared so that it fires only when invoked from OUTSIDE the package that owns it (where the package is installed as a vendored dependency). The trigger call site lives inside the owning package; the decision depends on the immediate caller's location. When the caller is outside the package boundary the deprecation fires; when the caller is itself inside the package it is treated as an internal call and stays silent. The owning package, identifier and message of the guarded deprecation are fixed by the scenario: package 'acme/widgets', identifier 'https://deprecations.example.com/widgets/42', message 'oldWidget() is deprecated, use newWidget() instead.'. The 'location' field selects where the caller sits relative to the package: 'outside' (consumer code) or 'inside' (the package calling its own deprecated path).

cases.expected_output:
event=deprecation_notice
backend=trigger_error
message=oldWidget() is deprecated, use newWidget() instead.
package=acme/widgets
link=https://deprecations.example.com/widgets/42
unique_count=1
count[https://deprecations.example.com/widgets/42]=1

feature8_disable_reset.json.description: Disabling the manager turns tracking off and resets the per-identifier counters to zero. Identifiers that were already seen remain listed but with a zero count, and the unique total drops back to zero.
